retrieved_docs.title: Ex utero Intrapartum Treatment (EXIT) Procedure -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The ex utero intrapartum treatment (EXIT) procedure represents a rare and high-risk intervention utilized to secure a fetal airway while maintaining placental support before birth. Typically performed for fetuses with congenital airway obstructions or cardiopulmonary malformations, EXIT aims to establish a definitive airway before delivery. This procedure encompasses various techniques, including airway establishment, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO), mass resection, and separation of conjoined twins. Due to its complexity, EXIT necessitates interprofessional teams and specialized tertiary care centers.

retrieved_docs.title: Placental Abruption -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis depends on when the patient presents to the hospital. If the bleeding continues, both maternal and fetal lives are at stake. Partial placenta separation is associated with low mortality compared to full separation; however, in both cases, without an emergent cesarean section, fetal demise may occur. The condition accounts for 5% to 8% of maternal deaths today.

retrieved_docs.title: Placental Abruption -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Women classified with a class 1 or mild placental abruption, no signs of maternal or fetal distress, and pregnancy less than 37 weeks gestation may be managed conservatively. These patients are usually admitted to the obstetrical unit for close maternal and fetus status monitoring. Intravenous access and blood work for type and cross-match are part of the plan of care. The maternal-fetal dyad continues to be monitored until there is a change in condition or fetal maturity.

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: On rare occasions, twin reverse arterial perfusion can occur. This condition leads to an abnormal fetus with the pathophysiology of being "parasitic" to the other twin's circulation. [9] Monochorionic, monoamniotic, and twin gestations can also occur if the embryo splits after seven days. Should pathologic embryonic cleavage occur after 14 days, there is a risk for conjoined twins. Another risk unique to monochorionic twin gestations is an unequal placental mass assignment due to the unequal placement of the vascular "equator" and the intervening membrane. The fetus with a smaller placental mass assigned carries a high risk for selective fetal growth restriction. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Ex utero Intrapartum Treatment (EXIT) Procedure -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The interventions known as ex utero intrapartum treatment (EXIT) are specialized procedures conducted in rare and high-risk cases where securing the fetal airway in utero becomes imperative before delivery. Typically, these procedures are undertaken in fetuses with congenital airway obstructions or cardiopulmonary anomalies that would otherwise lead to fatal outcomes. EXIT encompasses techniques such as establishing an airway, connecting to extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO), removing masses, or separating conjoined twins while the fetus remains supported by placental circulation. Given the complexity and rarity of these procedures, there are no established guidelines by medical societies emphasizing the need for specialized teams in tertiary care centers. Despite the potential risks involved for both the fetus and the mother, including failure to establish the airway or maternal hemorrhage, the collaborative efforts of interprofessional teams play a crucial role in mitigating these risks and ensuring successful outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Pregnancy Ultrasound Evaluation(Archived) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Another important goal of the routine obstetric ultrasound at 10 to 13 weeks of gestation is determining the number of fetuses, amnionicity, and chorionicity in twin gestations. The absence or presence of the intertwin membrane determines amnionicity. If no intertwin membrane is identified, pregnancy is described as "mono-amniotic and mono-chorionic," and it implies the highest potential rate of complications (cord entanglement and fetal death). [26] Chorionicity describes different types of placentation and also predicts risks in twin pregnancies. If intertwin membrane is identified, monochorionic or dichorionic placentation is investigated next. Monochorionic placentation has a single placenta, while dichorionic placentation describes 2 separate placentas in twin pregnancies. To determine chorionicity, the chorionic membrane base and thickness are evaluated. The presence of the lambda sign (a thick, Y-shaped base of the chorion) is used to predict chorionicity with greater than 95% accuracy, but only if it's used before 14 weeks of gestation. If the lambda sign is identified, a dichorionic pregnancy is diagnosed. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Twin Births (Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The care plan and evaluation for twin gestations depend on the definition of the chorionicity of the pregnancy. [13] A routine, early imaging policy would allow diagnosis of twin gestations and establish the chronicity reliably; however, the American College Obstetricians and Gynecology do not endorse routine ultrasound examination of the abdomen in fetal low-risk pregnancies. Identifying separate placentas is a highly reliable indicator of dichorionic twins; however, a unified placental appearance may represent a single placenta and a monochorionic twin gestation, although not necessarily so.

retrieved_docs.title: Placental Abruption -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Placental abruption is the early separation of a placenta from the lining of the uterus before the completion of the second stage of labor. It is 1 of the causes of bleeding during the second half of pregnancy. Placental abruption is a relatively rare but serious complication of pregnancy that puts the well-being of both mother and fetus at risk. It is also called abruptio placenta (See Image. Placental Abruption). [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Placental Abruption -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Placental abruption is the early separation of the placenta from the lining of the uterus before the completion of the second stage of labor. It is one of the causes of bleeding during the second half of pregnancy and is a relatively rare but serious complication that puts the well-being of both mother and fetus at risk. This activity describes the pathophysiology of placental abruption and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in managing affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Placenta -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The amniotic sac enlarges faster than the chorionic sac, which causes them eventually to come into contact and fuse into the amniochorionic membrane. The amniochorionic membrane then fuses to the decidua capsularis and, ultimately, the decidua parietalis for stability. The amniochorionic membrane ruptures during labor. The amniochorionic membrane with the fetal vessels makes up the chorionic plate. Parts of the decidua basalis grow into the chorionic plate dividing it into separated septa called cotyledons, in which each contains stem villi. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Placental Abruption -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical implications of a placental abruption vary based on the extent and location of the separation. Placental abruption can be complete or partial and marginal or central. The classification of placental abruption is based on the following clinical findings:

retrieved_docs.title: Placental Abruption -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Placental abruption is 1 of the causes of vaginal bleeding in the second half of pregnancy. A focused history and physical is critical to differentiate placental abruption and other causes of vaginal bleeding. Because a definitive diagnosis of placental abruption can only be made after birth when the placenta is examined, the history and physical examination are critical to the appropriate management of the maternal/fetal dyad. Placental abruption is a potentially life-threatening situation. Therefore, accurate patient assessment is critical to developing an appropriate management plan and preventing a potentially poor outcome.

retrieved_docs.title: Vanishing Twin Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: It is essential to examine the placenta after delivering the viable co-twin, to know about the morphologic characteristics of the lost twin. Although a vanished twin pregnancy can be noted via an ultrasound, no such histologic confirmatory evidence has been presented previously. [11] The results of studies after examination of the placenta ranged from histologically regular anembryonic gestational sacs to identifiable residues of fetal tissue. [12] In some cases, a mummified, compressed fetus in association with a viable fetus was observed, a condition called fetus papyraceous. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Delivery -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The final stage of labor includes the time after the child is born to the delivery of the placenta. The duration of this phase is approximately 30 minutes; [8] during this time, as the uterus contracts, the placenta separates from the endometrium. This process begins at the lower pole of the placenta, and progress is along with the adjacent sites of placental attachment. The continual uterine contraction causes a wave-like separation in the upward direction, which causes the most superior portion of the placenta to detach last. Signs of placental detachment include a sudden gush of blood, lengthening of the umbilical cord, and cephalad movement of the uterine fundus, which becomes firm and globular once the placenta detaches. The third stage of labor concludes once the placenta completely separates and is delivered. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Placental Abruption -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: There are no laboratory tests or diagnostic procedures to diagnose placental abruption definitively. However, some studies may be conducted to eliminate other conditions and provide baseline data. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography 3rd Trimester and Placenta Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Several measurements are indicated for appropriate assessment of the status of the fetus and the maternal structures, starting with the estimation of the gestational age. Gestation dating by ultrasonography may be inaccurate during the third trimester. [7] Measurements of fetal parts can be used in isolation or combined to estimate gestational age. Commonly used dating measurements include: Biparietal diameter (BPD) Occipitofrontal diameter (OFD) Head circumference (HC) Abdominal diameter (AD) Abdominal circumference (AC) Femur length (FL)

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Medical databases are clinically associated with a spectrum of obstetric conditions, serving as valuable resources for understanding and managing these entities. They are linked to مقدمات تسمم الحمل (early signs of preeclampsia), تسمم الحمل (preeclampsia), and الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (premature placental separation), providing data that aid in early detection, risk stratification, and therapeutic decision‑making for these serious pregnancy complications. In addition, databases are associated with تقلص الحوض (pelvic contraction) and تقلص مدخل الحوض (contraction of the pelvic inlet), offering information on uterine and pelvic dynamics that can influence labor progression and delivery planning. Finally, they are connected to gestation twin (twin pregnancy), supplying comprehensive records that support the monitoring of multiple fetuses, assessment of maternal‑fetal health, and coordination of specialized care. By integrating data across these conditions, medical databases enhance clinicians’ ability to deliver informed, evidence‑based obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine function includes several distinct conditions that are important for obstetric care: الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (early placental separation), رحم ناقص التوتر (uterus with insufficient tone), and عطالة رحمية ثانوية (secondary uterine inertia). These entities represent specific abnormalities of the uterus and placenta that can affect labor progression and fetal outcomes, and they are considered sub‑categories of overall uterine function in clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2261
retrieved_docs.content: الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (premature separation of the placenta) is clinically associated with بضع الرحم (uterine prolapse). This relationship indicates that women who experience بضع الرحم may have an elevated risk of developing الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة during pregnancy, a condition that can lead to significant maternal hemorrhage and fetal distress. Recognizing the link between بضع الرحم and الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة is important for obstetric surveillance and timely intervention to improve maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2388
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage during pregnancy, including the specific conditions abruptio placenta and placenta previa, are classified together as types of obstetric hemorrhage. This classification groups these distinct pregnancy‑related bleeding disorders, emphasizing their shared relevance to maternal health and guiding clinical assessment and management of hemorrhagic complications in pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Medical databases serve as clinically relevant resources for a range of obstetric conditions. They are linked to the study and management of الْتِهابُ المَشيماءِ والسَّلَى (inflammation of the placenta and membranes), حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ (a specific type of abnormal pregnancy), قلة السائل الأمنيوسي (oligohydramnios), نواتج متبقية من الحمل (retained products of conception), and gestation twin (twin pregnancy). By aggregating data on these entities, databases support diagnosis, risk assessment, and therapeutic decision‑making in maternal‑fetal medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: to the broader concept of fetal distress that influences maternal treatment (ضائقة جنينية تؤثر في علاج الأم), highlighting the bidirectional impact of fetal and maternal conditions. Maternal pregnancy complications as a whole are nested within a larger, unclassified group of maternal complications (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر), emphasizing that many of these disorders are interrelated and may require coordinated assessment, risk stratification, and management across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and post‑partum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2073
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY encompasses several serious obstetric conditions, specifically hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa. These three entities are classified as hemorrhagic complications of pregnancy, representing major sources of maternal morbidity and mortality. Recognizing that hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa fall under the broader category of pregnancy‑related complications highlights their interrelated nature and underscores the importance of prompt diagnosis and coordinated management in maternal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2081
retrieved_docs.content: Placenta circumvallata, placenta membranacea, placenta multipartita, tripartite placenta, and other placental disorders are all recognized as conditions that map to a high‑risk pregnancy. Conversely, a high‑risk pregnancy is associated with several specific placental and uterine abnormalities, including a double placenta, a displaced placenta without hemorrhage, placental necrosis, and uterine tumor. These interconnections emphasize that abnormal placental morphology and related uterine pathology are key factors in identifying and managing pregnancies classified as high risk, underscoring the need for careful monitoring and appropriate clinical intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2035
retrieved_docs.content: Circumvallate Placenta Syndrome is a condition that is directly related to the placenta. Both marginal placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage and partial placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage are identified as having the placenta as the primary site of pathology. The placenta itself can present in several anatomical configurations, including multipartite (multilobed) and tripartite (three‑lobed) forms, as well as a displaced placenta that occurs without bleeding. Pathological findings associated with the placenta encompass placental insufficiency, fetal‑maternal hemorrhage syndrome, and combined maternal and fetal hemorrhage. Recognizing these interconnections among placental disorders, their locations, and associated hemorrhagic complications is essential for accurate diagnosis and optimal obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 720
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is a broad category that includes several serious obstetric events: Hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa. These three conditions are closely linked, as each involves abnormal bleeding or separation of the placenta and can lead to or coexist with a displaced placenta, referred to in Arabic as مشيمة منزاحة. The presence of a مشيمة منزاحة often reflects the underlying pathology of hemorrhagic episodes, premature placental detachment (abruptio placenta), or abnormal placental implantation (placenta previa). Recognizing that Hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa are primary complications that may result in a مشيمة منزاحة helps clinicians prioritize early detection, appropriate monitoring, and timely intervention to improve maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1079
retrieved_docs.content: Perinatal Terminology comprises a specific subset of obstetric concepts that are essential for understanding and managing pregnancy and delivery. Within this subset are the terms الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (early placental separation), انْصِمامٌ بالسَّائِلِ السَّلَوِيّ (obstructive amniotic fluid condition), حمل بطني (abdominal pregnancy), حمل مطول (prolonged pregnancy), حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ (bicornuate uterus pregnancy), رحى عدارية خبيثة (malignant uterine wall lesion), قلة السائل الأمنيوسي (oligohydramnios), مشيمة ملتصقة (placenta accreta), and ولادة مهبلية (vaginal delivery). Recognizing each of these entities under the umbrella of Perinatal Terminology enables clinicians to identify high‑risk scenarios, tailor prenatal monitoring, and choose appropriate delivery strategies, thereby improving maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2248
retrieved_docs.content: Circumvallate Placenta Syndrome is directly associated with the placenta, indicating that this syndrome is a specific placental abnormality. Both Partial placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage and Total placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage identify the placenta as the primary site of their pathological findings, underscoring the central role of placental involvement in these hemorrhagic obstetric complications. The placenta itself can be described by several distinct locations and morphological variants: it may present as a “tripartita; placenta,” as a “مشيمة مزدوجة” (double placenta), or as a “مشيمة منزاحة من دون نزف” (displaced placenta without bleeding). In addition, the placenta can exhibit specific pathological findings, including “قصور المشيمة” (placental insufficiency), “نخر المشيمة” (placental necrosis), and “نزف في المشيمة” (placental hemorrhage). Together, these relationships highlight how various placental structural and functional abnormalities are linked to clinical syndromes such as circumvallate placenta and placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage, emphasizing the importance of detailed placental assessment in maternal‑fetal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2397
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ “مضاعفات وعوامل خطر الحمل والمخاض والولادة (باستثناء حالات الإجهاض وولادة جنين ميت)” (Complications and Risk Factors of Pregnancy, Labor, and Delivery, excluding miscarriage and stillbirth) encompasses a range of obstetric conditions that are important for clinical assessment and coding. Included in this SMQ are “رحى عدارية خبيثة” (malignant placental tumor), “الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة” (early placental separation), “رأب عنق الرحم” (cervical stenosis), “انحباس المشيمة أو الأغشية، من دون نزف” (retained placenta or membranes without hemorrhage), “حمل بطني” (abdominal pregnancy), and “رضح توليدي آخر، غير ذلك” (other obstetric delivery). By classifying these entities within the same SMQ, they are recognized as significant pregnancy‑related complications that can affect maternal and fetal health, guide risk assessment, and support appropriate patient management and health‑care reporting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2441
retrieved_docs.content: Placenta; fenestrata, placenta; membranacea, placenta; multipartita, and اضطراب المشيمة are all conditions that map to حمل عالي الخطورة (high‑risk pregnancy). Conversely, حمل عالي الخطورة is linked to several serious obstetric complications, including قصور المشيمة (placental insufficiency), مشكة مزدوجة (double placenta), مشيمة منزاحة مع نزف (displaced placenta with bleeding), نزف الأم والجنين (maternal and fetal hemorrhage), and ورم الرحم (uterine tumor). The presence of these specific placental abnormalities therefore signals an increased likelihood of a high‑risk pregnancy, and a high‑risk pregnancy should prompt careful surveillance for the listed complications to optimize maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 247
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a spectrum of obstetric conditions centered on placental and uterine pathology. Circumvallate Placenta Syndrome and other specific placental morphologies—placenta circumvallata, placenta fenestrata, placenta membranacea, placenta multipartita, spurious placenta (spuria placenta), tripartite placenta (tripartita placenta), double placenta (مشيمة مزدوجة), and displaced placenta with (مشيمة منزاحة مع نزف) or without hemorrhage (مشيمة منزاحة من دون نزف)—are identified as placental disorders (اضطراب المشيمة) that confer high‑risk pregnancy (حمل عالي الخطورة). Associated placental findings include placental insufficiency (قصور المشيمة), transfusion syndrome via the placenta (متلازمة انتقال الدم بطريق المشيمة), placental necrosis (نخر المشيمة), maternal and fetal hemorrhage (نزف الأم والجنين), and hemorrhage within the placenta (نزف في المشيمة). Placenta previa in its marginal, partial, and total forms, each with intrapartum hemorrhage, as well as displaced placenta

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2232
retrieved_docs.content: Circumvallate Placenta Syndrome is directly linked to the placenta, indicating that this syndrome involves placental pathology. The placenta can exhibit several distinct morphological locations, including a fenestrated form (“placenta; fenestrata”) and a multipartite form (“placenta; multipartita”). It may also be found in a displaced position (“مشيمة منزاحة”), with a variant in which the displacement occurs without accompanying hemorrhage (“مشيمة منزاحة من دون نزف”). Pathological findings associated with the placenta encompass “متلازمة انتقال الدم بطريق المشيمة” (a syndrome of blood transfer through the placenta), placental necrosis (“نخر المشيمة”), and bleeding that involves both the mother and the fetus (“نزف الأم والجنين”). Recognizing these anatomical variations and pathological sites is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and management of pregnancies affected by Circumvallate Placenta Syndrome.
